contents.value:
```ruby
puts(*objects) -> nil
```
Writes the given objects to STDOUT, followed by a newline for each. Calls `.to_s` on each object. Returns `nil`.

```ruby
print(*objects) -> nil
```
Prints the given objects to STDOUT without a trailing newline. Calls `.to_s` on each object.

```ruby
p(*objects) -> object
```
For each object, calls `object.inspect` and writes the result to STDOUT followed by a newline. Returns the object (or array of objects if multiple).

```ruby
pp(*objects) -> object
```
Pretty-prints the given objects with nice formatting and indentation. Returns the object.

```ruby
printf(format_string, *args) -> nil
```
Prints a formatted string to STDOUT using format specifiers like `%s`, `%d`, `%f`, `%x`.

```ruby
warn(*messages, uplevel: nil, category: nil) -> nil
```
Writes the given messages to STDERR. If `uplevel` is given, prepends the file and line info.

```ruby
def method_name(args)
  body
end
```
Defines a new method. The return value is the result of the last evaluated expression, or an explicit `return`. Methods are instances of `UnboundMethod`.

```ruby
class ClassName < SuperClass
  body
end
```
Defines a new class. Classes are open and can be reopened to add or modify methods. Inherits from `Object` by default.

```ruby
module ModuleName
  body
end
```
Defines a module. Modules serve as namespaces and as mixins. They cannot be instantiated. Use `include` to mix into a class.

```ruby
include(module, ...) -> self
```
Includes the given module(s) into the current class or module. The module's methods become available as instance methods.

```ruby
extend(module, ...) -> self
```
Adds the methods from the given module(s) as singleton (class-level) methods to the receiver.

```ruby
prepend(module, ...) -> self
```
Prepends the module(s) to the current class, inserting them before the class in the method lookup chain. Useful for method wrapping.

```ruby
require(name) -> true or false
```
Loads the given library (a `.rb` or native extension). Returns `true` if loaded successfully, `false` if already loaded. Searches `$LOAD_PATH`.

```ruby
require_relative(path) -> true or false
```
Loads a file relative to the directory of the file containing the `require_relative` call.

```ruby
attr_accessor(*symbols) -> array
```
Defines getter and setter instance methods for the named attributes. Equivalent to calling both `attr_reader` and `attr_writer`.

```ruby
attr_reader(*symbols) -> array
```
Defines getter instance methods for the named attributes. Each generated method returns the value of the corresponding instance variable.

```ruby
attr_writer(*symbols) -> array
```
Defines setter instance methods for the named attributes. Each generated method sets the corresponding instance variable.

```ruby
raise(exception_class, message, backtrace)
raise(message)
raise
```
Raises an exception. With no arguments, re-raises the current exception. With a string, raises a `RuntimeError`. With a class, creates and raises an instance of that class.

```ruby
yield(*args) -> object
```
Transfers control to the block passed to the current method. Passes the given arguments to the block and returns the block's return value.

```ruby
block_given? -> true or false
```
Returns `true` if a block was passed to the current method via `yield`. Useful for methods that optionally accept blocks.

```ruby
lambda { |args| body } -> Proc
```
Creates a lambda (a Proc with strict argument checking). `return` inside a lambda exits the lambda only. Also available as `->` (stabby lambda).

```ruby
proc { |args| body } -> Proc
```
Creates a Proc object. Unlike lambdas, procs have relaxed argument checking and `return` exits the enclosing method.

```ruby
if condition
  body
elsif condition
  body
else
  body
end
```
Conditional execution. In Ruby, only `false` and `nil` are falsy; everything else (including `0` and `""`) is truthy. `if` is also an expression that returns a value.

```ruby
unless condition
  body
else
  body
end
```
Negated conditional. Executes the body if the condition is `false` or `nil`. Equivalent to `if !condition`. Avoid using `unless` with `else`.

```ruby
case expression
when value1
  body1
when value2
  body2
else
  default_body
end
```
Case expression using `===` for comparison. Supports ranges, classes, regexps, and procs as `when` values. Ruby 2.7+ also supports pattern matching with `case/in`.

```ruby
while condition
  body
end
```
Executes the body repeatedly while the condition is truthy. Can also be used as a modifier: `do_something while condition`.

```ruby
until condition
  body
end
```
Executes the body repeatedly until the condition becomes truthy. Equivalent to `while !condition`.

```ruby
begin
  body
rescue ExceptionClass => e
  handle error
else
  no error occurred
ensure
  always runs
end
```
Exception handling block. `rescue` catches exceptions, `else` runs when no exception occurs, `ensure` always runs (like `finally` in other languages).

```ruby
rescue ExceptionClass => variable
```
Catches exceptions in a `begin/end` block or method body. Without a class, catches `StandardError`. Multiple `rescue` clauses are checked in order.

```ruby
ensure
  cleanup_code
end
```
Code in the `ensure` clause always runs, whether or not an exception was raised. Similar to `finally` in Java/JavaScript.

```ruby
self -> object
```
Refers to the current object (the receiver of the current method call). At the top level, `self` is `main`. Inside a class body, `self` is the class.

```ruby
super
super(args)
```
Calls the same-named method in the superclass. Without parentheses or arguments, passes along the current method's arguments. With explicit arguments, passes those instead. With empty parens `super()`, passes no arguments.

```ruby
obj.freeze -> obj
```
Prevents further modifications to the object. Attempting to modify a frozen object raises `FrozenError`. Freezing is permanent and cannot be undone.

```ruby
obj.frozen? -> true or false
```
Returns `true` if the object is frozen (cannot be modified). All `Integer`, `Float`, `Symbol`, and frozen string literals are frozen.

```ruby
collection.each { |element| block } -> collection
collection.each -> Enumerator
```
Calls the given block once for each element in the collection, passing that element as a parameter. Returns the collection itself.

```ruby
collection.map { |element| block } -> Array
collection.map -> Enumerator
```
Returns a new array with the results of running the block once for every element in the collection. Alias: `collect`.

```ruby
collection.select { |element| block } -> Array
collection.select -> Enumerator
```
Returns a new array containing all elements for which the block returns a truthy value. Alias: `filter`.

```ruby
collection.reject { |element| block } -> Array
collection.reject -> Enumerator
```
Returns a new array excluding all elements for which the block returns a truthy value. Opposite of `select`.

```ruby
collection.reduce(initial) { |accumulator, element| block } -> object
collection.reduce(symbol) -> object
```
Combines all elements by applying a binary operation. Also known as `inject` or `fold`. Without an initial value, uses the first element.

```ruby
collection.flat_map { |element| block } -> Array
```
Returns a new array with the concatenated results of running the block for every element. Equivalent to `map` followed by `flatten(1)`.

```ruby
collection.group_by { |element| block } -> Hash
```
Groups the collection's elements into a hash. Keys are the block's return values; values are arrays of elements that produced that key.

```ruby
collection.sort_by { |element| block } -> Array
```
Sorts the elements using the values returned by the block as sort keys. Uses a Schwartzian transform for efficiency.

```ruby
collection.tally -> Hash
collection.tally_by { |element| block } -> Hash
```
Counts the occurrences of each element and returns a hash where keys are elements and values are their counts. Added in Ruby 2.7.

```ruby
File.read(filename, length = nil, offset = 0, **opts) -> String
```
Opens the file, optionally seeks to the given offset, reads `length` bytes (or all if nil), and returns the data as a string.

```ruby
File.write(filename, string, offset = nil, **opts) -> Integer
```
Opens the file, optionally seeks to the given offset, writes the string, and returns the number of bytes written.

```ruby
File.open(filename, mode = 'r', **opts) -> File
File.open(filename, mode = 'r', **opts) { |f| block } -> object
```
Opens the file with the given mode. When a block is given, the file is passed to the block and automatically closed afterward.

```ruby
File.exist?(path) -> true or false
```
Returns `true` if the named file exists. Follows symlinks.

```ruby
File.delete(*filenames) -> Integer
```
Deletes the named file(s). Returns the number of files deleted. Raises `Errno::ENOENT` if a file does not exist.

```ruby
File.join(*parts) -> String
```
Joins the given path components using `File::SEPARATOR` (typically `/`). Handles redundant separators.

```ruby
File.dirname(path, level = 1) -> String
```
Returns all components of the filename except the last one. With `level`, strips that many trailing components.

```ruby
File.basename(path, suffix = '') -> String
```
Returns the last component of the filename. If `suffix` is given and present, it is removed from the result.

```ruby
Dir.glob(pattern, flags = 0, base: nil) -> Array
```
Returns filenames matching the given glob pattern. Supports `*`, `**`, `?`, `[set]`, and `{alt1,alt2}` syntax.
